🟥 WHAT HAPPENS IF SARA DUTERTE RESIGNS BEFORE IMPEACHMENT? 

There's a rumor going around that Sara Duterte might resign to avoid impeachment, keep herself eligible for 2028, and step off the stage before Congress finally turns against her completely. 

Still just rumor. But the questions behind it are real. So let's break it down.

🟥 THE STARTING POINT: 215 VOTES AND A SUPREME COURT THAT SAID NO

Let me set the stage. On February 5, 2025, 215 members of the House endorsed the fourth impeachment complaint against Sara Duterte. That's way more than the 102 votes you need to move something to the Senate. 

The House was serious. They had the votes. They were ready to put her on trial.

The complaint wasn't about nothing. 

It centered on P612.5 million in confidential funds she controlled as Vice President and DepEd secretary. 

The allegations: misuse, plunder, graft. Money that was supposed to go somewhere—or someone—ended up nowhere. 

Ghost expenses. Fictitious names on documents. Hundreds of millions paid out to people who don't exist on paper.

But before the Senate even got started, the Supreme Court stepped in. 

July 25, 2025. A 13–0–2 vote. The Court said no—the impeachment violated the one-year rule. 

You can't file two impeachment complaints against the same person in the same calendar year. 

So they killed it. But they also said clearly: any new complaint could restart after February 6, 2026.

So we ended up here. 

215 House members said yes. The Supreme Court said not like this. And now the rumor is that Duterte might just resign before it happens again.

🟥 THE LEGAL MESS: DOES IMPEACHMENT DIE IF SHE WALKS?

Here's where it gets messy. The Constitution says impeachment has two penalties: removal from office and disqualification from public office. It doesn't say what happens if you resign before the Senate votes.

So lawyers are split. Retired Senior Associate Justice Antonio Carpio says the case becomes moot. If she's already gone, there's nothing left to remove. The trial ends. No disqualification. She can run in 2028 from an impeachment standpoint—legally clean.

Other constitutional experts, like Prof. Tony La Viña, say no. The Senate can keep going for disqualification alone. Even if removal is pointless, the question of whether she's fit for office again still counts. The Constitution lists two separate penalties. One goes moot, fine. But the other one is still on the table.

The UP College of Law's impeachment primer admits this is unresolved. There's no clear answer because the Constitution doesn't address it. And Carpio himself said if the Senate tries to continue after resignation, Duterte can go to the Supreme Court and ask if they even have the power. The Court would have to decide something it's never decided before. That's what "case of first impression" means—first time anyone's asking this question.

In other words: the law doesn't settle this. Politics does.

🟥 THE P612.5 MILLION, THE OMBUDSMAN, AND WHAT REALLY MATTERS

Impeachment is one layer. Criminal liability is another. And resignation doesn't touch the second one.

The P612.5 million is the symbol here. But it's not symbolic at all. 

House investigations and complaints describe real money supposedly disbursed through Duterte's office between 2022 and 2023. 

The allegations say budgeting rules were violated. Documentation was fake or missing. Money went out to people who cannot be traced.

Civil society groups, religious leaders, ordinary citizens filed plunder, graft, bribery complaints with the Ombudsman. 

In June 2025, the Ombudsman ordered her to respond. That means the investigation is moving. These complaints specifically invoke the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act and the Plunder Law. 

If convicted, you don't just lose your job. You lose the right to hold any public office forever. And you can go to prison.

There's also the joint BPI account. The one Rodrigo and Sara Duterte allegedly share. 

Reports say around P2.2 billion flowed through it. Huge deposits never declared in their asset disclosures. That raises questions about unexplained wealth. Anti-graft rules. Money laundering rules.

And earlier on, when someone like Samuel Martires held key positions in the system, graft cases would simply vanish. Disappear. No conviction, no acquittal. Just gone. 

That pattern is critical because it tells you how the system has worked when the powerful were accused.

If the Ombudsman finds probable cause, the cases go to the Sandiganbayan—the anti-graft court. 

And here's the key: resignation doesn't stop that. Even if she walks away from the VP office, the Sandiganbayan can still try her. 

Conviction for plunder means imprisonment, forfeiture of assets, and perpetual disqualification from public office. Not just removal. Not just disqualification. Jail.

But the catch is time. Ombudsman cases take years. Criminal trials take longer. 

By 2028, you might not have a conviction yet. And that's the point of resignation. 

Leave office now, claim the process was political, and campaign in 2028 while the criminal system grinds slowly in the background.

🟥 THE SENATE, ROBIN PADILLA, AND THE ART OF DOING NOTHING

When 215 House members voted to impeach her on February 5, 2025, they didn't guarantee conviction. They just guaranteed a trial would happen. In the Senate, you need two-thirds to convict. That's 16 out of 24 senators.

Early reporting made clear Duterte had a loyal bloc. Robin Padilla and others aligned with her father's political network. These senators could slow-walk the trial, block conviction, or just drag it out until people stop paying attention.

The House showed appetite for impeachment. The Senate did not. And Senate President Chiz Escudero made sure of it. 

He used procedure, caution, Supreme Court delays, other legislative priorities as reasons to archive the complaint. 

Talk about due process. Talk about ensuring fairness. 

But the practical effect: the case went nowhere. It was delayed, shelved, and protected by Senate leadership. 

Observers saw it clearly. Senate leadership was running interference for Duterte. Using institutional language to avoid taking a stand.

Later, Senate President Tito Sotto echoed the same rhetoric. More caution. More process. Same result.

So Duterte watched this unfold. She saw the House cross 215 votes once. She saw the Supreme Court bail her out on technical grounds but also make clear new complaints could restart after February 6. She saw the Ombudsman order her to respond to P612.5 million allegations. She saw Senate leadership delay indefinitely.

From that angle, resignation before a second impeachment cycle fully starts looks less like rumor and more like obvious strategy. 

Avoid the televised trial. Reduce the risk of a conviction vote. Reposition yourself as someone who stepped aside. Keep 2028 open.

🟥 2028: THE REAL GAME

Almost every serious person analyzing Duterte's moves comes back to one date. 2028. The election.

If there's no impeachment conviction, the Senate never votes on disqualification. 

If she resigns and the case is treated as moot, there's no impeachment-based bar to running again. 

If criminal cases are filed but not decided by the time candidates file their candidacy papers, the pending cases don't automatically keep you off the ballot. 

Only a final conviction for offenses that carry perpetual disqualification does that.

That's why some scholars and even prosecutors publicly argued the Senate should continue impeachment trials after resignation. 

Reach a verdict on disqualification. Set a precedent that resignation isn't an escape hatch. 

But that requires things we can't assume: a Senate willing to push back against someone powerful, and a Supreme Court willing to uphold that interpretation instead of calling it moot.

In Philippine political culture, powerful people have always found ways out. Technical dismissals. One-year bars. favorable rulings. So trusting that maximum accountability will happen is not a safe bet.

🟥 THE DUTERTE PATTERN, THE ICC, THE JOINT ACCOUNTS

When people talk about accountability for the Duterte family, they're not just talking about one impeachment. They're looking at a broader pattern.

Rodrigo Duterte faces international scrutiny over killings linked to the drug war. The International Criminal Court is discussing whether these were crimes against humanity. Domestic courts haven't moved on it decisively.

And then there's the money. The P2.2 billion in the joint BPI account. P612.5 million in confidential funds. Civil society groups filing plunder complaints related to her time as Davao City mayor and as DepEd secretary. Multiple accusations. Multiple allegations. Multiple points where money is supposed to appear and doesn't.

From a distance, impeachment, confidential funds, joint accounts, ICC investigations—they don't look like separate storms. They look like one system. 

A family and network repeatedly bumping into the borders of accountability. Repeatedly testing how far institutions will bend before they snap.

🟥 THE PART THAT MATTERS: THE VICTIMS

Here's what gets lost when we're talking about legal theories and constitutional ambiguity. 

The P612.5 million came from taxpayers. It was meant for schools. For teachers. For students. Real people depending on resources that supposedly went somewhere and instead went nowhere.

Resignation doesn't make that question disappear. It doesn't return the money. It doesn't answer where it went. It moves the question to a slower system where time and fatigue work in Duterte's favor. 

Criminal cases take years. By then the story is old. The news cycle moved on. The people who needed that money have already waited too long.

Accountability is gone in favor of the powerful names. 

🟥 SO IF SHE RESIGNS, WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENS?

On the impeachment side, the Constitution's silence means the Senate and eventually the Supreme Court decide what happens. 

One reading says the case becomes moot and dies. Another says the Senate can continue for disqualification. If they try, the Supreme Court gets called in to draw the line.

On the criminal side, resignation does nothing. The Ombudsman still investigates. The Sandiganbayan can still try her. Criminal conviction carries imprisonment and perpetual disqualification—harsher than impeachment but slower to reach.

Electorally, if there's no impeachment conviction and no final criminal conviction with disqualification by the time 2028 candidacy filing opens, she remains legally eligible to run. That's the quiet power of resignation. Step out before a verdict. Claim the process was political. Keep the door open for 2028.

And at the institutional level, the message becomes clear. If resignation kills impeachment, if the Senate doesn't push back, if criminal cases drag on without resolution, then future officials learn one thing: there's always an exit. You just have to time it right.

🟥 WHEN "IMPOSSIBLE DREAM" SOUNDS LIKE GIVING UP: SOTTO'S STATEMENT AND WHAT IT SAYS ABOUT LEADERSHIP  

When I read Senate President Tito Sotto calling impeachment "an impossible dream" after the Supreme Court ruling, my first reaction was: okay, I get the frustration. The highest court just told Congress—again—that their impeachment process against Vice President Sara Duterte was unconstitutional. Unanimous decision. Final.

Sotto's anger was sharp. He called it "a clear encroachment on the power of the Legislative branch." He said the Court engaged in "judicial legislation." He warned it would "take decades of retirements to correct this misinterpretation."

On the constitutional question, I think he might have a point. The Constitution gives the House the exclusive power to initiate impeachment and the Senate the sole power to try and decide cases. That language feels deliberate. Exclusive. Sole.

But the more I mull over it, the more another question bothers me: when does defending institutional boundaries start to look like surrender? When does righteous indignation about process become an excuse not to find another path forward?

Because from where I sit—just someone who reads too much and drinks too much coffee—"impossible dream" doesn't sound like institutional defense. 

It sounds like giving up.

🟥 WHAT THE SUPREME COURT ACTUALLY DID  

Let me try to break down what happened, because I had to read this ruling a few times to understand it.

The Court used the one-year bar rule in the Constitution: no impeachment proceedings can be initiated against the same official more than once within a year. 

The justices said the first complaint against Sara Duterte in December 2024 already triggered that bar. 

So the fourth complaint, filed and transmitted to the Senate in February 2025, violated the one-year rule.

But here's where it gets complicated—and where I think Sotto's complaint has some weight.

The Court went beyond the text. It said those first complaints should have been included in the House Order of Business within 10 "session days." 

Then it interpreted "session days" to mean any calendar day that the House holds a session. I can't find that definition in the Constitution itself. The Court seems to have added it.

So Sotto's anger? It comes from this: the judiciary just wrote new procedural rules for how Congress must handle impeachment—rules not spelled out in the Constitution.

Now, I'm not a constitutional lawyer. I could be missing something. But from what I can tell reading the ruling and the analysis around it, that's the core issue.

🟥 WHY SOTTO'S POSITION MAKES SENSE — UP TO A POINT  

Sotto isn't someone I'd dismiss lightly.

He's been in the Senate since the early 1990s. He was Senate President before, and he's back in that role now. And here's something that stood out to me: when the Senate voted 19–4 to archive Sara Duterte's impeachment last year, Sotto was one of the four who voted against archiving it. He didn't join the majority that shelved the case. He backed accountability in that moment.

So when he gets angry about the Supreme Court stepping into impeachment territory, I don't think it's automatically about protecting Sara personally. It fits a longer pattern of him defending the Senate's independence.

The legal debate here goes back decades. Traditionally, impeachment was seen as a "political question"—something courts should stay away from. 

The 1987 Constitution changed the terrain by allowing courts to review acts of any branch for "grave abuse of discretion." That opened the door for the Court to step into impeachment when Congress acts in ways it sees as unconstitutional.

Where exactly that line should be is still messy. I don't have a clean answer. Does reviewing an impeachment for basic constitutionality still respect the "exclusive" and "sole" powers of the House and Senate? Or does it turn the Court into a gatekeeper?

Sotto stands on that line and says: the Court crossed it.

And honestly? I think that's at least worth arguing.

🟥 BUT HERE'S WHERE "IMPOSSIBLE DREAM" LOSES ME  

All of that is fair to debate. But here's where I get stuck.

Leadership isn't only about being right on principle. It's also about what you tell the public to expect next.

When Sotto called impeachment "an impossible dream," he wasn't just criticizing the Supreme Court. He was signaling to the country—and to his own institution—that accountability for impeachable officials is now out of reach.

That is where his statement turns from institutional defense into something that feels like defeat.

Sometimes I picture the impeachment process like a small, battered boat. 

The one-year bar rule is the hole that’s always been there in the hull. It was part of the design from day one. 

What the Supreme Court did was hammer in a wooden plug and declare, more or less, that this is it: this is how the hole will work from now on. 

No more creative patching. No more trying to work around it.  

And yet, if there’s anything history keeps teaching us, it’s that power always looks for gray areas. Rules get bent. Processes get stalled. Loopholes appear. 

So when Sotto says impeachment is now an “impossible dream,” I don’t just hear legal frustration. I hear someone deciding not to even look for the next gap in the wall.

Maybe I'm reading too much into two words. But "impossible dream" doesn't leave room for "here's what we do next." It closes the door.

Realism accepts that a door is blocked. Defeatism insists that all doors are blocked. The difference is whether you still talk about the next move.

I keep thinking: he could have said, "We disagree with the ruling. We will revise our rules to comply. When a new complaint is filed after the one-year ban expires, we will do our constitutional duty."

Instead, he jumped straight to "impossible dream."

From someone holding the Senate's highest post, that doesn't just describe the situation. It shapes it.

🟥 WHAT SOTTO COULD HAVE SAID — BUT DIDN'T  

Here's the thing: there is already a clear path forward.

The one-year ban lifts on February 6, 2026. Lawmakers from the Makabayan bloc and others have said they plan to refile an impeachment complaint against Sara Duterte once that date passes. 

The House has publicly signaled it's ready to accept new complaints. Members of the House prosecution team have talked about revising the rules to match the Supreme Court's interpretation so the next complaint survives procedural scrutiny.

Even Sara Duterte seems to expect it. Her camp says they've been preparing for more impeachment attempts and that complaints will keep coming until her term ends.

In other words: the game isn't over. It's paused and moving to a stricter rulebook.

A Senate President who wants accountability would say: "Fine. We will meet those rules. We will be ready."

A Senate President who says it's all an impossible dream is doing something different. The message to the public is simple: don't expect anything.

And maybe that's just political realism. Maybe Sotto knows his colleagues won't vote to convict anyway, so why pretend?

But I can't shake the feeling that when leaders at that level declare something impossible, it becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy.

🟥 SOTTO'S RESPONSIBILITY AS SENATE PRESIDENT  

This is where his role matters, and where I might be expecting too much—but I'll say it anyway.

The Constitution doesn't just say the Senate can try impeachment. It says it has the sole power to try and decide these cases, and that trials should "proceed forthwith" once articles are transmitted.

When Sara Duterte's impeachment first reached the Senate, that didn't happen. The case was debated, stalled, then archived. The Supreme Court later killed the complaint on constitutional grounds. But the Senate itself already showed reluctance before that.

Now the head of that same institution is telling the public impeachment is impossible.

That feels like more than a legal opinion to me. That feels like an institutional mood signal. It tells senators, parties, and even potential complainants in the House that the upper chamber isn't inclined to fight for this tool anymore.

From a Senate President, that sounds less like defense of the Constitution and more like advance notice of non-use.

Maybe I'm wrong. Maybe there are constraints I don't see from where I sit. But that's how it reads to me.

🟥 THE COST OF DEFEATISM  

I've watched enough Philippine scandals to recognize a pattern.

Big scandal. Loud outrage. Investigations. Then delay. Technicalities. Tiredness. Eventually, people forget.

Each time, there's a moment when institutions could choose to keep pushing. Each time, there's also someone powerful saying, in different words: "Wala na. Tapos na yan."

Sotto's "impossible dream" line sits in that space.

Coming from him, it doesn't just predict difficulty. It normalizes giving up. It offers cover for inaction: if even the Senate President thinks it can't be done, why should anyone else waste political capital trying?

That is how impunity hardens. Not only through bad rulings, but through leaders who tell people to stop expecting more.

🟥 WHY THIS MATTERS FOR 2028  

I might be connecting dots that don't connect. But hear me out.

Sara Duterte is widely expected to be a key player in 2028, maybe even a presidential contender. The impeachment complaints against her revolve around questions of confidential funds, abuse of power, and open threats against other officials.

The Supreme Court ruling didn't clear her on the substance. It only said this particular impeachment move was unconstitutional on timing and procedure. But politically, it still looks like a win for her.

When the Senate President adds "impossible dream" to that mix, it feeds the talking points. Not necessarily one he wants, but one that still benefits her. It whispers to the public: if you want justice, don't look here.

For someone who says he cares about the Senate's power, that has real consequences. It shapes how 2028 will be fought, and who walks into that race without any formal reckoning on record.

🟥 MY UNCOMFORTABLE CONCLUSION  

I could be wrong about all of this. I'm not a lawyer. I'm not in the Senate halls hearing the side comments and quiet deals. I'm just someone trying to connect what’s on paper with what it signals to the rest of us.

But here's the point I keep coming back to: 

Tito Sotto is probably right about one big thing—the Supreme Court stepped closer to Congress's turf than many of us are comfortable with. Expanding procedural requirements beyond the Constitution's clear text should worry anyone who cares about separation of powers.

Defending that line is important work.

But calling impeachment an "impossible dream" goes beyond that. It folds constitutional critique into a broader message of resignation. It blurs the line between "this decision is wrong" and "there is nothing more we can do."

He didn't have to choose that framing. He could have attacked the ruling and still committed to future action.

Instead, indirectly, he told the country: don't expect accountability.

From a senator at the back row, that would just be disappointing. From the Senate President, it feels dangerous. It teaches people to live with impunity as a permanent condition.

As a citizen who demands accountability from those who are accused of corruption, it frustrates me. 

But that's exactly when leadership is supposed to resist the urge to give up—not give language to it.

When the Supreme Court first blocked the impeachment case against Sara Duterte in July, I was angry. 

I wrote about it. I said what many of us were thinking: here we go again, another institution closing ranks to protect someone close to power.

The ruling was 13-0. All unanimous. 

With a Court packed with 12 Duterte appointees, it was hard not to see it as a protection job. 

The Court said the fourth impeachment complaint violated Article XI, Section 3(5) of the Constitution—the one-year rule. 

You can't start more than one impeachment process against the same official within a year. 

The first three complaints came in December 2024. 

The fourth, with over 250 House signatures, landed in February 2025. 

That was the problem.

At that moment, I didn't care about the technicality. I cared that someone facing serious questions about confidential funds was getting off the hook. 

Or at least, that's how it looked from where I was sitting with my coffee.

🟥 WHAT I'VE BEEN THINKING SINCE THEN

Since that first ruling, I've had time to cool down and read more. 

I still don't like the composition of that Court. But I see more clearly now what the decision actually did.

The one-year rule wasn't invented for Sara. It was already in the Constitution before this case. 

On January 28, 2026, the Court upheld its original ruling and denied the House's motion for reconsideration. No more appeals. No more "let's ask again." The decision is final.

That move didn't erase the suspicion. But it did settle one big question: are they really barred forever, or just for a fixed period of time?

🟥 WHY THIS ACTUALLY OPENS A DOOR

The House has been stuck. They filed the complaint. The Court said it broke the one-year rule. They tried to get the Court to change its mind. The Court refused. So now we are left with the calendar.

The first set of complaints was treated as "initiating" impeachment in early February 2025. That means the one-year bar runs until February 6, 2026. 

After that date, a new complaint is allowed. Not because the Court is kind, but because that's how the Constitution is written.

So what does that mean in real life? 

If the lawmakers who signed that big complaint are serious, they now have a specific date to work with. No more hiding behind confusion. 

They can spend the months before February tightening the allegations, checking every procedural step, and making sure the next filing doesn't get thrown out on a technical issue again.

On paper, it looks like Sara "won" this round. 

In practice, the people who want her impeached now have a clearer path: wait out the one-year bar, then file properly, with timing and process fully in order.

🟥 THE DDS SHADOW OVER THE COURT

We also need to be honest about the other side of this. The Supreme Court is dominated by Duterte appointees. 

A daughter of the former president walks into a Court heavily shaped by that same president. People have every reason to doubt.

That doesn't mean every ruling they make is automatically wrong. But it does mean every ruling involving the Dutertes will carry a stain in the public mind. Even when the legal logic is plain and simple, the politics around it is loud.

Some retired justices and legal voices said the Court should have moved more slowly here. 

They wanted more hearings, more openness, more visible care before striking down impeachment articles backed by more than 250 lawmakers. 

When a Court is this loaded with one family's appointees, it should be extra careful. It wasn't.

So where do I land on this? 

I don't see this Court as neutral. I think it's carrying baggage from the last administration. 

At the same time, the one-year rule explanation lines up with how the Constitution is written. 

Both can be true at once: the Court is politically tainted, and this specific reading of the timing rule still stands on solid text.

🟥 WHERE I STAND NOW

When that first ruling came out, I saw a solid wall. Like the system had found a clean-looking way to say "no" to any serious attempt at holding Sara Duterte accountable.

Now, after the January 28 decision denying the motion for reconsideration, I see something a bit different. 

Not a friendly Court. Not a fair system. But a small opening that is very clear: after February 6, 2026, the timing excuse disappears. 

If there is no new impeachment effort after that, the blame can't sit only with the Court anymore.

Which is why, if there is anything I am watching, it's the count of the congressmen and women who will sign the next impeachment complaint. They had over 250 signatures last year. Let's see if they can hold that number this year.

I'm still tired. I'm still wary of institutions shaped by DDS power. But I'm also not ready to stop paying attention. 

The House has a date, a process, and a chance to try again with fewer technical mistakes.

If those more than 250 lawmakers truly believe Sara Duterte should face an impeachment trial, the next move is on them. 

They can use this clearer window and file again when the bar expires, or they can keep pointing at the Court and do nothing.

That choice will tell us more than any press release ever will.

🟥 WHY AREN'T WE DOING ANYTHING ABOUT TRAITORS IN THE GOVERNMENT?

I keep seeing that word thrown around. Traitor.

It came up again this January. Three Filipino fishermen were injured on December 13 when Chinese Coast Guard vessels attacked them with high-pressure water cannons off Escoda Shoal. Their boats were damaged. It was the first time China targeted fishermen directly at close range. The water cannon hit them hard enough to cause open wounds.

Then in late January, the Chinese Embassy attacked Philippine officials on social media for criticizing the aggression.

The National Security Council filed "firm representations"—basically a formal complaint. Fifteen senators signed Senate Resolution 256 condemning the Chinese Embassy's "undiplomatic" remarks and affirming the Philippines' sovereign right to defend its position.

But nine senators refused to sign.

Eight from the minority bloc did not sign: Alan Peter Cayetano (Senate Minority Leader), Christopher "Bong" Go, Imee Marcos, Ronald "Bato" dela Rosa, Francis "Chiz" Escudero, Robin Padilla, Rodante Marcoleta, and Joel Villanueva.

One from the majority bloc did not sign: Pia Cayetano.

Only Jinggoy Estrada from the minority signed the resolution.

Senator Robin Padilla did not sign. Instead, he criticized PCG spokesperson Jay Tarriela for using caricatures of Xi Jinping in presentations, calling it "improper" for a uniformed official to mock a foreign president. He said "Pag nambastos ka, babastusin ka rin"—if you disrespect, you'll be disrespected. He said he would defend even President Marcos if another country mocked him. He acknowledged his views could invite accusations of being "pro-China," but said he was willing to accept such criticism if it meant advocating for peace and de-escalation.

Senator Rodante Marcoleta did not sign either. He met with Chinese Ambassador Huang Xilian. He echoed the ambassador's complaint that China is being portrayed as the "villain." He said: "Parang kami naman masyadong kontrabida"—mimicking the envoy, "It's like we're too much of the bad guy." He suggested the Philippines could share the West Philippine Sea with China. He criticized the government's transparency initiative for causing "panic." He claimed Tarriela didn't consult with the DFA before speaking publicly. He's the same senator who previously claimed the West Philippine Sea didn't exist when he was in the House of Representatives.

And people lost it.

Social media exploded. Senator Risa Hontiveros responded directly: "It's time for us to really know who is profiting, who is amplifying, and who is China's megaphone here in the Philippines. If they are determined to work against their own people, that is no longer a strategy. That is treason against the Motherland."

PCG Commodore Jay Tarriela said something similar back in 2023: "If you are a Filipino, whether in government or private sector, regardless of your politics, defending and making excuses for China's aggressive behavior should deem you unpatriotic, and a traitor to the Philippines and to our people."

The anger is real. The frustration is earned. People are watching senators defend the country that just injured three Filipino fishermen. They're watching politicians dressed up cowardice as diplomacy, pandering as pragmatism, appeasement as peace advocacy.

But here's the thing: calling someone a traitor and actually prosecuting them for treason are two completely different situations. And the gap between those two realities explains why we're not seeing anyone jailed for what many Filipinos believe is treason.

So let me walk through what treason actually means under Philippine law, why prosecutions almost never happen, and what alternatives exist when the legal definition of treason doesn't cover the betrayal people are witnessing.

🟥 WHAT TREASON ACTUALLY MEANS UNDER PHILIPPINE LAW

Treason is defined in Article 114 of the Revised Penal Code, as amended by Republic Act 10951. The law is clear but strict.

Two ways to commit treason:

1. Levying war against the Philippines
2. Adhering to enemies of the Philippines, and giving them aid or comfort—either inside the country or abroad

Who can be charged:

- Filipino citizens (owing permanent allegiance)
- Resident aliens (owing temporary allegiance while living here)

The punishment:

- Filipino citizens: reclusion perpetua (life imprisonment) to death, plus a fine up to ₱4,000,000
- Resident aliens: reclusion temporal to death, plus a fine up to ₱4,000,000

That sounds serious. And it is. 

But...yes, there is a big BUT.

🟥 THE REQUIREMENTS THAT MAKE TREASON ALMOST IMPOSSIBLE TO PROSECUTE

Philippine law doesn't just define treason. It builds massive walls around it to prevent political persecution disguised as treason charges.

First requirement: There must be a state of war.

Treason is a war crime. It cannot be committed during peacetime.

Justice Gregorio Perfecto said it in the 1947 case Laurel v. Misa: "While there is peace, there are no traitors. Treason may be incubated when peace reigns. Treasonable acts may actually be perpetrated during peace, but there are no traitors until war has started".

The Philippines has to be an active participant in a war. Not just tensions. Not just maritime harassment. Not even armed skirmishes. Actual war.

The problem? We're not at war. 

China's aggression in the West Philippine Sea—water-cannoning our fishermen, ramming our boats, occupying our reefs—does not legally constitute war under the Revised Penal Code. 

It's hostile. It's illegal under international law. 

But it's not war in the legal sense that would trigger treason charges.

This is the wall that stops everything. 

No matter how many senators defend China, no matter how many times officials criticize Philippine sovereignty, there is no legal basis for a treason prosecution without an actual state of war.

Second requirement: Two witnesses must testify to the same overt act, or the accused must confess in open court.

This is the two-witness rule. And it's brutal.

You can't convict someone of treason based on:
- One witness
- Circumstantial evidence
- Extrajudicial confessions (like admissions made outside court)
- Different witnesses testifying to different acts

Both witnesses have to see the same specific treasonous act. If Witness A saw the accused give money to the enemy on Monday, and Witness B saw the accused give weapons on Tuesday, that doesn't count. They didn't witness the same overt act.

The only alternative is if the accused confesses in open court during arraignment, pre-trial, or trial. But obviously, accused traitors rarely stand up and admit their crimes in front of a judge.

This standard was designed to protect citizens from politically motivated accusations. 

Treason has historically been used by authoritarian governments to silence opposition. The framers of our laws wanted to make sure treason convictions required overwhelming, undeniable proof.

But that same protection makes real prosecutions nearly impossible.

🟥 THE ONLY TIME THE PHILIPPINES SUCCESSFULLY PROSECUTED TREASON

There's one period in Philippine history when treason prosecutions actually worked: right after World War II.

Between 1947 and 1949, the newly independent Republic of the Philippines conducted 73 trials through the People's Court against 155 individuals—mostly Filipino collaborators who worked with the Japanese occupation forces.

Examples:

- Susano Perez (People v. Perez): convicted for recruiting Filipino women to serve Japanese officers
- Apolinar Adriano (People v. Adriano): convicted for membership in the Makapili, a pro-Japanese paramilitary organization

These cases worked because:

1. There was a clear state of war (World War II, Japanese occupation)
2. Witnesses could testify to specific overt acts (recruiting women, wearing enemy uniforms, serving in enemy forces)
3. The acts occurred during active hostilities between the Philippines and Japan

By December 28, 1949, 138 individuals had been convicted and 79 sentenced to death. 

Eventually, many of the accused received amnesty as part of post-war reconciliation. But the legal framework functioned because all the elements of treason were present.

We haven't seen anything close to that since.

🟥 WHY MODERN ACCUSATIONS OF TREASON GO NOWHERE

Let me give you the most common examples people point to.

Duterte's "gentleman's agreement" with China

In 2024, former President Rodrigo Duterte admitted he made a secret agreement with Chinese President Xi Jinping not to repair or resupply structures in the West Philippine Sea. 

Critics called it treason. Some said he gave away Philippine territory.

Legal reality: No war = no treason prosecution possible under Article 114.

Could it qualify as something else? Maybe "betrayal of public trust," which is an impeachment ground under the Constitution. But treason? No. The law doesn't allow it.

Trillanes' backchannel talks with China

In 2016, Duterte accused Senator Antonio Trillanes IV of treason for conducting backchannel negotiations with China during the Scarborough Shoal standoff in 2012. Duterte said Trillanes' trips to Beijing led to the loss of the shoal.

Legal reality: Diplomacy—even backchannel diplomacy—is not treason. There was no war. Trillanes didn't levy arms against the government or give aid to an enemy during wartime. It was a political accusation with no legal basis.

Sara Duterte and confidential funds

Vice President Sara Duterte is now facing impeachment for alleged misuse of confidential funds and making threats against President Marcos. People call it betrayal. Some call it corruption. A few call it treason.

Legal reality: The impeachment charges are based on "betrayal of public trust," "graft and corruption," and "culpable violation of the Constitution"—not treason. Because again, treason requires war.

🟥 WHAT THE LAW USES INSTEAD OF TREASON

Since treason is nearly impossible to prosecute in peacetime, the Philippine legal system relies on other mechanisms to hold officials accountable.

1. Betrayal of Public Trust (Impeachment Ground)

This is one of the grounds for impeachment under Article XI, Section 2 of the 1987 Constitution.

It's broader than treason. It covers "acts just short of being criminal but constitute gross faithlessness against public trust, tyrannical abuse of power, inexcusable negligence of duty, favoritism, and gross exercise of discretionary powers".

It doesn't require war. It doesn't require two witnesses to the same overt act. It's a political process, decided by Congress.

Used in:
- Chief Justice Renato Corona's impeachment (2012)
- Allegations against Rodrigo Duterte over the Pharmally scandal
- Current impeachment proceedings against Sara Duterte

2. Graft and Corruption

Republic Act 3019, the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, covers:
- Using public office for private gain
- Awarding contracts to favored parties
- Accepting bribes
- Causing undue injury to the government

The evidentiary standards are lower than treason. Cases go to the Sandiganbayan, the anti-graft court. Conviction rates are low—around 46% according to one study—but prosecutions happen.

3. Sedition and Rebellion

Articles 134-142 of the Revised Penal Code cover sedition, insurrection, and rebellion.

Rebellion means "rising publicly and taking arms against the Government for the purpose of removing allegiance to said Government". It doesn't require war with a foreign power. It's about internal armed uprisings.

Sedition involves creating public disturbances or inciting people to rise against the government.

Both have lower thresholds than treason and don't require the two-witness rule.

🟥 WHY ACCOUNTABILITY STILL FAILS EVEN WITH THESE ALTERNATIVES

The problem isn't just that treason is hard to prove. The problem is that the entire accountability system in the Philippines is weak.

Slow prosecution

Cases take an average of 4.6 years from filing to decision in the Sandiganbayan. By the time a case is resolved, the accused may have already moved on, gained more power, or died.

Political protection

Officials from powerful families rarely face real consequences. Networks of implicated politicians, bureaucrats, and contractors protect each other. Investigations happen. Reports get published. But conviction? That's rare.

Weak enforcement

The Office of the Ombudsman is supposed to investigate and prosecute corrupt officials. But under some administrations, it has been accused of protecting allies instead of prosecuting them.

Former Ombudsman Samuel Martires, a Duterte appointee, repeatedly cleared Duterte of wrongdoing and dismissed lifestyle checks as "illogical". Institutional capture weakens accountability before cases even reach court.

Impeachment is a numbers game

Impeachment is a political process. Even if there's evidence, conviction requires a two-thirds vote in the Senate. If the accused has political allies, they survive. If they don't, they fall.

President Duterte's spokesperson Salvador Panelo said it himself: impeachment is "merely a numbers game".

🟥 SO WHY AREN'T WE DOING ANYTHING ABOUT TRAITORS?

Because the law defines treason narrowly. Because we're not at war. Because the two-witness rule creates an almost insurmountable evidentiary wall.

And because even when other charges—graft, betrayal of public trust, corruption—could work, the system is too slow, too weak, and too compromised to deliver justice consistently.

People feel betrayed. They watch senators refuse to condemn China's attack on Filipino fishermen. They watch politicians defend the country that just injured three of our fishermen. They watch officials dress cowardice as diplomacy, pandering as pragmatism, appeasement as peace advocacy. The anger is real. The word "traitor" feels right.

But the law doesn't care about feelings. It cares about elements, evidence, and procedure. And under those rules, treason prosecutions in peacetime simply don't happen.

What we call treason in everyday language is often prosecuted—if it's prosecuted at all—as something else: impeachment for betrayal of public trust, graft under RA 3019, or sedition if there's armed uprising.

The harder question is whether those mechanisms actually work. Based on conviction rates, case backlogs, and the number of powerful officials who retire peacefully despite credible allegations, the answer is: not nearly enough.

I’ve been monitoring the FDA situation for months now because this isn’t abstract to me—I take 14 maintenance pills a day, and I need to know that every single one is real, safe, and properly regulated. 

At the same time, I keep reading accounts from small business owners, health workers, and ordinary buyers saying the same things: the process is brutal, fake products are everywhere, and government action feels painfully slow. 

Part of my own advocacy is simple: if I can’t raid warehouses or file cases, at least I can help clean up the market by sharing information and connecting the dots in public.

So when this latest Senate hearing happened—Risa Hontiveros chairing, the two Tulfo brothers grilling FDA chief Paolo Teston—it felt like those scattered worries I’d been seeing finally showed up in an official transcript. 

And when you line that up with Raffy Tulfo’s longer history with the FDA, his brand deals, and the way our e-commerce laws work now, the picture gets even more complicated.

🟥 A COUNTRY OF 110 MILLION, AND 248 INSPECTORS

There’s one number I can’t get over: 248.

That’s the number of FDA inspectors in the Field Regulatory Operations Office. They’re supposed to cover Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao. 

That’s the entire country: over 110 million people, thousands of pharmacies, groceries, neighborhood stores, warehouses, and now countless online listings across several platforms.

Roughly, that’s one inspector for more than 400,000 people. And that’s just people—not even counting the number of outlets and products. In the hearing, FDA officials themselves admitted they are short on both people and money.

At the same time, those 248 inspectors are expected to deal with:

- Counterfeit Biogesic, Decolgen, Neozep, and other basic medicines in ordinary stores.  
- Imported products in Binondo and Quiapo that have had advisories against them for years.  
- Weight-loss and “sleeping” products containing amphetamine.  
- Prescription-only drugs being sold openly online without prescriptions.

Even if everyone in the FDA were honest and efficient, the design alone makes it almost impossible to keep up.

🟥 SMES STUCK IN THE MIDDLE

While all of this is happening, small and medium enterprises are stuck in a choke point.

To legally sell pre-packaged food, supplements, or cosmetics, you need a License to Operate (LTO) and a Certificate of Product Registration (CPR). 

On paper, the charter says an LTO should be processed in about 20 days. In reality, businesses report:

- LTO applications dragging for 1–2 years.  
- CPR renewals taking 3–5 years, even when there are no formulation changes.

Fees have also jumped. For some food supplement categories, registration that used to cost around ₱1,000 is now closer to ₱15,000. For a micro or small business trying to launch a few SKUs, that’s a major hit.

On top of that, if you want to get into groceries or pharmacies, or if you want to export, those chains and buyers will usually demand valid FDA CPRs. So SMEs end up in this bind:

- Without FDA approval, they can’t enter modern retail or export.  
- With slow approvals and high fees, they burn capital while watching bigger brands—with ad budgets and celebrity endorsers—take the shelf space.

That’s the context behind all the headlines: there’s an entire layer of businesses waiting in line while the enforcement and political battles unfold above them.

🟥 THOUSANDS OF COMPLAINTS, ALMOST NO CONSEQUENCES

Then there’s the part that really shows how weak the system is: thousands of complaints, but almost nothing serious happens to the people behind the products.

In the hearing, FDA officials said roughly 3,000 complaints have been filed over the years, but only 14 cases actually made it to court, all ending in fines instead of real jail time. 

The main law they rely on, the FDA Act of 2009, is built for administrative penalties—suspending licenses, canceling authorizations, and imposing fines that can go as low as tens of thousands of pesos. 

The heavier punishments, like long prison terms and multi-million peso fines under the Special Law on Counterfeit Drugs, only really kick in when you can prove a product is truly counterfeit and tie it to serious harm or death.

So most of what we call “violations” live in this grey zone: serious enough to scare consumers, but not always packaged neatly enough to fit strict criminal standards. 

That’s where legislation comes in. 

This is exactly why Hontiveros is pushing a bill to treat big counterfeit operations as economic sabotage—because as long as the law keeps most of these cases in the “just pay a fine and move on” bucket, the people making money off dangerous products have no real reason to stop.

🟥 ADVISORIES THAT STAY ON PAPER

The hearing also made clear that an advisory on paper doesn’t automatically clean up the market.

Take Hokkaido slimming/sleeping pills. 

The FDA issued an advisory against this product back in 2013, warning that it contained amphetamine,  a powerful stimulant.

In 2026, Raffy Tulfo’s staff still managed to walk into stores and buy Hokkaido products. He then brought them to the Senate hearing as props.

So we’re looking at a thirteen-year gap between the advisory and that hearing, with the product still on shelves. That spans multiple directors and multiple administrations. This is not a “Teston-only” story.

The same pattern is there with counterfeit versions of everyday medicines like Biogesic, Decolgen Forte, and Neozep. Warnings exist, but you can still buy fake versions in the wild.

🟥 ONLINE: A WHOLE DIFFERENT MESS

And that’s just the physical stores. Online is its own battlefield.

During the hearing, senators showed that you can easily buy prescription-only medicines on Shopee, Lazada, or even via TikTok without ever showing a prescription.

Some of the examples mentioned:

- Prescription-only cardiac and hypertension drugs being sold on e-commerce platforms with no prescription requirement.  
- Unregistered versions of Tirzepatide popping up on TikTok, even though only Mounjaro is FDA-registered in the Philippines.

That means people can treat social media and shopping apps as their pharmacy, without doctors, diagnostic work, or proper dosing. That opens the door to wrong doses, bad drug combinations, and antibiotic resistance.

Erwin Tulfo pointed out that agencies like the PNP, PDEA, and NBI have large intelligence budgets and should be watching these online markets too, not just focusing on physical drug operations.

🟥 THE FDA’S SIDE OF THE STORY

To their credit, the FDA did not just sit there and say nothing.

Paolo Teston and his team brought numbers:

- In just two months, they said they seized more than ₱56 million worth of violative and counterfeit health products.  
- Over an eleven-year period, they claimed total seizures of more than ₱860 million.  
- In that recent two-month period, they said they monitored around 3,307 listings and removed 1,531 from platforms.

Teston denied that the agency tolerates corruption and said he is open to investigations, but he won’t resign. 

He also reminded everyone that he only took over in May 2025 and that the agency is operating with very limited staff and funding.

Raffy Tulfo countered by pointing to the public-facing data: he said only 970 listings had actually been taken down, and that the Oplan Katharos takedown page hadn’t been updated since March 2025.

So yes, the FDA is doing something, but compared to how big and old these problems are, it still looks small and slow.

🟥 PLATFORMS MOSTLY WAIT FOR COMPLAINTS

The hearing also put Facebook, TikTok, Shopee, and Lazada on the record.

Their message boiled down to:

- They have policies against counterfeit and unregistered health products.  
- They take down listings that clearly break those rules.  
- But they rely heavily on user reports and complaints for enforcement.

In other words, no one is systematically checking every listing against FDA approvals. If no one complains, a questionable product can stay up and keep selling.

Hontiveros pointed out that these platforms earn money from this marketplace, but carry almost no risk when bad products slip through, and she argued they should be held more responsible.

🟥 IT’S NOT JUST FDA VS PLATFORMS: DTI IS NOW IN THE PICTURE

There’s also a new layer most people don’t know about: by law, it’s not just FDA and the platforms anymore. DTI now has formal power over e-commerce and online marketplaces.

In 2023, Congress passed the Internet Transactions Act. Under this law:

- DTI must create an E-Commerce Bureau that will oversee the e-commerce industry.  
- That bureau is tasked to monitor compliance of e-marketplaces, digital platforms, and online merchants with the law.  
- It must build an Online Business Database of platforms and merchants.  
- It tracks complaints on internet transactions and refers them to the proper regulatory agencies like the FDA.

The law is very clear: DTI has regulatory jurisdiction over the use of the internet for conducting e-commerce by e-marketplaces, online merchants, and digital platforms, on top of whatever powers specialized regulators already have.

So the division of labor looks like this:

- FDA decides whether a health product is registered, safe, or violative.  
- DTI, through the E-Commerce Bureau, can pressure platforms and merchants—order takedowns, keep blacklists, impose administrative fines—when those products are being sold online.  
- PNP, NBI, PDEA, DOJ are the ones with the power to make arrests and bring criminal cases to court.

DTI also has teeth on paper: it can issue takedown orders, blacklist non-compliant sites, accounts, and apps, and impose fines up to ₱1 million on platforms and merchants that violate the law.

So the question isn’t just “Why isn’t the FDA fixing this?” 

It’s also:

- How is DTI using its new powers over platforms?  
- How are FDA, DTI, and law enforcement coordinating so that takedowns, seizures, and arrests actually line up?

Right now, it feels like each agency is doing its own thing in its own lane, and the gaps are exactly where counterfeit and unregistered products slip through.

🟥 TULFO VS THE FDA: LONGER TIMELINE, BIGGER INTERESTS

Now, there’s another layer that’s hard to ignore: this is not the first time Raffy Tulfo has gone after the FDA, and his relationship with the pharmaceutical/supplement world is not neutral.

When he entered the Senate in 2022, he brought with him long-standing accusations about corruption and bribery in the FDA, and those attacks only escalated once he had a formal platform in hearings and budget debates. 

In one budget hearing, he claimed that “bribery” and “conflict of interest” were plaguing the FDA and said he had sources telling him that companies were paying millions per product for registration or renewal. 

He named former FDA chief Nela Charade Puno, saying she admitted there was corruption in the registration process and tried to clean up the agency before she was fired. 

He also flagged then-cosmetics director Ana Trinidad Rivera for having a sibling at Zuellig, calling that a conflict of interest.

By 2025, under Paolo Teston, Tulfo’s line hardened further:

- He slammed the FDA in hearings for the continued sale of unregistered and dangerous products and said the FDA chief should resign if those products remained in the market.  
- He reminded Health Secretary Ted Herbosa about a supposed promise to fire Teston if Tulfo could get those products taken down faster than the FDA.  
- In late January 2026, he openly told Teston to resign “if you can’t fix the agency.”

He sometimes talks as if he has helped force out multiple FDA heads. But when you place that against the actual timeline, the story looks more modest:

- Nela Charade Puno was fired in 2019 by President Duterte, years before Tulfo became senator.  
- Samuel Zacate served from 2022 to May 2025 and left when Paolo Teston was appointed; there’s no clear public record that Tulfo caused his exit.  
- Teston is the first FDA chief he is actively trying to unseat while he is in office.

So the clash between Tulfo and the FDA has history, but the idea that he has personally “removed” past director-generals doesn’t quite match the sequence of events.

🟥 AT THE SAME TIME, HE’S THE FACE OF AN FDA-REGULATED BRAND

Here’s where the conflict of interest comes in.

Raffy Tulfo is not just a critic of the FDA. He is also a long-time brand ambassador for ATC Healthcare, a company that lives and dies by FDA regulation.

We’ve seen:

- ATC Healthcare’s own posts calling him “Robust Extreme’s ambassador,” featuring him with the company’s president.  
- Coverage of ATC’s 10th-year celebration describing him as the Robust Extreme endorser.  
- Radio and online ads labeled as ATC VitaCinc spots, with him promoting their Vitamin C + Zinc product.

ATC Healthcare sells food supplements and similar products, including Robust, Robust Extreme, and VitaCinc, all of which are regulated by the FDA and depend on FDA registration to be on the market legally.

So on one side, you have a senator who:

- Earns money by promoting products from a pharmaceutical/supplement company.  
- Depends on FDA approval and renewal processes for those products to keep selling.

On the other side, the same senator:

- Regularly accuses FDA of being “one of the most corrupt agencies.”  
- Claims that big pharmaceutical players can pay millions to fast-track registration.  
- Publicly pressures the regulator that oversees his sponsor’s product line.

It doesn’t mean ATC did anything wrong. It doesn’t automatically prove he’s interfering for them. But it does raise a fair question: how do you square being a paid face of an FDA-regulated brand with being the loudest political attacker of the agency that regulates your sponsor?

It becomes even more awkward when you remember that he has pushed for laws against conflicts of interest in other sectors—calling out doctors who receive perks and commissions from pharma companies, and arguing those relationships should be disclosed and regulated.

If doctors need to be transparent about financial ties to pharma, what about senators who endorse pharma or supplement brands while grilling the regulator in public hearings?

🟥 WHAT NEEDS TO HAPPEN NEXT

After months of watching this and reading the hearing transcripts, it feels like we’re dealing with two different layers:

- On one hand, the system is clearly broken:  
  - 248 inspectors for an entire archipelago.  
  - Around 3,000 complaints, 14 cases, all ending in fines.  
  - Old advisories that don’t translate into consistent follow-through.  
  - SMEs stuck in multi-year queues while big brands move faster.  
  - Online platforms that mostly wait for complaints.  
  - A new e-commerce law that gives DTI power over platforms, but no obvious, fully coordinated play between DTI, FDA, and law enforcement.

- On the other hand, most of the political heat is aimed at one man’s resignation, delivered by someone who is also a paid endorser for an FDA-regulated company.

If the point is to actually protect people, not just score points, then the direction has to refocus toward:

- Passing stronger laws that treat large-scale counterfeiting as economic sabotage and raise the stakes for offenders.  
- Increasing FDA’s staffing and budget so “248 inspectors” becomes something closer to a realistic number.  
- Making full use of the Internet Transactions Act: forcing platforms to do more than react—demanding proactive checks, clearer liability, and effective takedowns via DTI’s E-Commerce Bureau.  
- Making law enforcement agencies use their intelligence funds on online drug markets, not just physical ones, and actually bringing more cases to court.  
- And finally, enforcing real transparency around financial ties to pharma and supplements, whether you are a doctor, a regulator, or a senator.

Without moves like that, changing FDA directors is just changing the nameplate on the door. 

The fake and unregistered products will still be on shelves, still be in your feed, and still be a few taps away—and the people paying the price will remain the same: small businesses trying to play by the rules, and ordinary consumers who just want to trust what they’re taking.

🟥 THE SENATORS WHO KEEP BENDING TO CHINA

Fifteen senators managed to sign a resolution telling the Chinese Embassy to stop attacking Filipino officials who defend our seas. 

Fifteen. That’s it. 

In a country of over a hundred million people being pushed around in its own waters, only fifteen members of the Senate had the basic courage to say, “You don’t get to bully our people on our soil.”

The rest? Alan Peter Cayetano, Bong Go, Imee Marcos, Rodante Marcoleta, Ronald “Bato” dela Rosa, Robin Padilla, Francis “Chiz” Escudero, Joel Villanueva, and Pia Cayetano quietly stepped aside. 

They want you to believe this is “prudence” or “diplomacy.” 

It’s not. 

It’s moral cowardice masquerading as strategy.

🟥 THE DUTERTE BAGGAGE BLOCK

Most of these senators are not mysterious figures with neutral foreign‑policy minds. 

They are Duterte baggage — the same gang that spent years kneeling to Beijing and telling Filipinos that this was “smart.”

Bong Go is Duterte’s shadow, not a statesman. 

His job, from the start, has been to protect Duterte’s myth. 

If he signs a resolution that calls China out as a bully, he also admits Duterte’s China pivot was a humiliating failure. 

He would rather let Filipino officials and fishermen twist in the wind than sign his name to that admission.

Bato dela Rosa talks like a tough guy (sic) until the subject is China. 

Suddenly, the man who ran a bloody drug war becomes a soft‑spoken apologist. China “respected” us, he says, while our fishermen were being chased away and our reefs were being cemented over. 

Now that the Chinese Embassy is spitting on our officials in public, Bato’s advice is to pray. 

Pray — because actually standing up to the bully is apparently a bridge too far for the senator who once bragged he was willing to “die” for this country.

Alan Peter Cayetano, Duterte’s former foreign secretary, built an entire doctrine around not embarrassing Beijing. 

He downplayed the arbitral victory, called our own waters “disputed,” and treated public outrage as the real threat, not Chinese ships. 

Now, when the Senate is asked to sign a document that explicitly confronts the Chinese Embassy, Cayetano suddenly goes missing. 

Of course he does — the resolution exposes the bankruptcy of the quiet, submissive line he has defended for years.

🟥 DIALOGUE AS A FIG LEAF FOR SURRENDER

Then there are the kunyari “sophisticated” ones: Imee Marcos and Chiz Escudero.

Imee loves to talk about peace accords and long‑term arrangements with China, comparing us to India managing its border with Beijing. 

It sounds clever until you remember one thing: China is not “managing” anything in the West Philippine Sea; it is actively stealing. 

Chinese ships are blocking our boats, blasting them with water cannons, and driving our people out of our own fishing grounds — and Imee’s primary concern is avoiding “escalation.” 

Escalation for whom? The only ones escalating are on China’s payroll.

Chiz Escudero likes to warn against moves that might “box in” talks with China. 

But somehow, the only people he ever worries about boxing in are us.

China boxes in our fishermen, boxes in our coast guard, boxes in our future, and Chiz’s big fear is a piece of paper saying, “Stop attacking our officials.” 

Chiz is a politician who has grown comfortable with the idea that Filipinos must always be the ones to shrink, period. 

🟥 MARCOLETA AND PADILLA: ECHOING THE BULLY

Rodante Marcoleta and Robin Padilla are in a league of their own.

Marcoleta meets with China’s ambassador and comes back parroting Beijing’s lines almost word for word. 

Suddenly, China is the one being “unfairly” cast as a villain. 

Suddenly, our government being honest about Chinese harassment might “cause panic.” 

Suddenly, the West Philippine Sea — our sea — is something we should consider “sharing.” 

Sharing what, exactly? 

Our dignity? Our fishing grounds? Our spine? 

The only thing he never suggests sharing is accountability.

Robin Padilla, meanwhile, saves his outrage not for Beijing, but for Commodore Jay Tarriela, the man risking his safety and career to tell Filipinos the truth about what’s happening out there. 

Padilla’s problem is not that Chinese vessels block our boats or hound our fishermen; his problem is that Xi Jinping’s feelings might get hurt. 

If you’re more offended by memes of a dictator than by the abuse of your own people, you are not a nationalist. 

You are a volunteer doormat.

🟥 VILLANUEVA AND PIA: KNOW BETTER, DO LESS

Joel Villanueva and Pia Cayetano are not stupid. They know exactly what this resolution means and what refusing to sign it signals.

Villanueva chose his corner. 

He stuck with a minority bloc led by Cayetano and padded with Duterte allies instead of breaking ranks to stand with Filipino officials under attack. 

That’s the choice: bloc comfort over backbone.

Pia, sitting in the majority, watched almost all her colleagues sign and decided her brand — that carefully curated image of “independent” caution — was more important than adding her name to a clear stand against foreign bullying. 

She could have tipped the scales of history a tiny bit toward courage. 

Instead, she chose silence, which always, always tilts toward the oppressor.

🟥 THE VIEW FROM THE BOAT

Imagine a fisherman from Zambales, staring at the sea he’s worked all his life, deciding whether to risk sailing near Scarborough Shoal again. 

He remembers the last time — the Chinese Coast Guard cutting him off, horns blaring, bigger vessels muscling his small boat out of the way like he was garbage. 

He went home almost empty, with debts piling up and his kids asking why there was less food on the table.

He hears the news that the Chinese Embassy has attacked a Filipino official for speaking up. 

He also hears that the Senate was asked to sign a resolution condemning that attack. Only fifteen said yes. 

The others — many of them Duterte’s men and women — ducked.

That fisherman may not know all their names, but he understands one thing better than anyone in a barong: out on the water, you’re either with the people in the small boat or with the ships that push them away. There is no neutral.

These senators chose the ships.

🟥 CALL IT WHAT IT IS

Stop calling them cautious. Stop calling them neutral. Stop pretending this is nuance.

When a foreign embassy humiliates Filipino officials for defending Philippine waters and you refuse to sign a simple resolution condemning that, you are not being “careful.” 

You are siding with the bully. 

You are protecting a failed Duterte-era fantasy that being nice to China would save us, even while Filipinos are shoved out of their own seas.

These senators are not standing in the middle. They are standing behind China, using our flag as a prop.

🟥 WHY WE ALL NEED TO KEEP CRITICIZING CHINA  

[An MCT Commentary]

China is throwing another tantrum over the Philippines calling out its behavior in the West Philippine Sea. Embassy statements, water cannon videos, carefully worded DFA notes, press releases from select lawmakers—if you watch them in sequence, a pattern appears. Every time you put Beijing under pressure, it reacts. And the reaction itself is the confession.

When I say “keep criticizing China,” I don’t mean childish name‑calling or fake toughness. I mean describe what they’re doing in plain language, show the receipts, refuse to sand down the edges so their propaganda can glide over it. 

For a system that depends on darkness and polite fictions, that already feels like an attack. 

And every time they push back, they tell on themselves.

🟥 WHAT HAPPENS WHEN YOU PUSH BACK  

Look at how they behave with bigger players.

In Europe, the EU and China spent about seven years negotiating a big investment agreement, closing it at the end of 2020. Then the EU sanctioned a few Chinese officials over abuses in Xinjiang—limited and symbolic, more moral line than economic weapon. 

China answered by sanctioning European parliamentarians and institutions, including the very people who had to approve the deal. The European Parliament froze ratification. 

So one overreaction killed a deal Beijing had worked on for years, just because they couldn’t swallow even mild accountability.

In Australia, the script is similar. In 2020, Canberra called for an independent inquiry into the origins of COVID‑19. 

Beijing decided to teach them a lesson: tariffs, restrictions, and informal bans hit more than ten Australian export sectors, from barley and wine to coal. 

But the worst‑case economic collapse never came. Markets rerouted, other buyers stepped in, and Australia did not reverse its position. 

Instead, the episode pushed Canberra deeper into security cooperation with other democracies and hardened public opinion against China.

In both cases, the pattern is simple: thin skin, heavy punishment, long‑term damage to themselves. 

This is how power behaves when it cares more about being feared than being trusted.

🟥 THE NATIONALISM CORNER BEIJING PUT ITSELF IN  

Under Xi, China has been telling its people one story again and again: we were humiliated, we’re rising, and we will never bow to foreigners again. It’s emotionally effective. It gives people pride and a clear villain.

But that story comes with a trap.

Once you tell 1.4 billion people that losing face in front of the world is unacceptable, even modest criticism starts to look like an existential threat. 

A visit like Nancy Pelosi’s trip to Taiwan in 2022 doesn’t just become “a diplomatic disagreement.” 

It becomes a wound to national pride that the leadership has to answer with visible toughness—military drills, hardline statements, more pressure around Taiwan—just to show they’re living up to their own narrative.

There’s research showing that even in an authoritarian system like China’s, people react badly when leaders talk big and then back down. 

That’s what political scientists call “audience costs”: the price a government pays at home when its threats turn out to be empty. 

In China, those costs aren’t the same as in a Western democracy, but they’re real enough that leaders feel them. So they escalate the talk, and then feel forced to escalate the actions.

Wolf warrior diplomacy grew out of this environment. 

Younger, aggressive diplomats started throwing sharp lines on Twitter, lecturing foreign journalists, and clapping back at any perceived insult to China. 

It was a clear break from the older “keep your head down” approach. 

At first, it impressed nationalists and made them feel China was finally “standing up.” 

Over time, though, it backfired: Canada, Sweden, Australia, many in Europe and Asia saw it as bullying and overreach. 

The “peaceful rise” image cracked.

In short: Beijing built a nationalism machine that now dictates its foreign policy reactions. 

They’re not just responding to us. They’re performing for their own crowd.

🟥 WHAT THE PHILIPPINES IS DOING DIFFERENTLY  

Now, zoom back to the West Philippine Sea.

Since early 2023, the Philippines has leaned into a transparency strategy. 

Instead of burying incidents in diplomatic notes and vague pressers, the government started releasing videos, photos, and timelines whenever Chinese ships used water cannons, did dangerous maneuvers, or blocked resupply missions. 

That laser incident near Ayungin was a turning point: from then on, the instinct was “ipakita na yan.”

This does three important things.

First, it exposes China’s favorite tactics—gray‑zone operations designed to push boundaries without triggering all‑out war. Those tactics work best when most people don’t see them or don’t understand what’s going on. When you put them on screen and explain them, it becomes harder for Beijing to claim they’re just “protecting their territory” in a calm sea.

Second, it lines up with what Filipinos actually want. Surveys in recent years show strong support for a firmer stance on the maritime disputes, and Marcos Jr. has enjoyed relatively high approval on this issue. People don’t want war, but they do want someone to stand up and stop pretending nothing is happening.

Third, it closes the space for shady “understandings.” When everything is quiet, there’s room for undocumented promises, alleged verbal deals, and arrangements that never see the light of day. Then, years later, China says, “pero nag‑usap na tayo dati, remember?” and nobody can prove or disprove it. Public documentation—photos, dates, coordinates, videos—turns each incident into something that can’t easily be rewritten.

The key: transparency only works if it’s consistent. 

You can’t expose a water cannon one month and then keep the next one secret because Beijing smiled in a meeting. 

As long as China behaves, there’s nothing to show. The moment there’s something to show, hiding it only helps them.

🟥 WHY A REGIME LIKE THIS HATES LIGHT  

Authoritarian regimes thrive on things the public doesn’t see.

If you look at China’s overseas projects, you see a pattern: opaque contracts, hidden loan terms, unclear ownership structures. 

Many Belt and Road problems—debt traps, half‑finished ports, domestic backlash in partner countries—stem from deals that would never have survived real parliamentary or public scrutiny. 

They were built in the dark, and they fell apart in the light.

Democracies have ugly sides, but they do have one built‑in advantage: if people keep talking and reporting, some truth escapes. 

Strategic communication, when done right, is just democracy using its own strengths. Not copy‑pasting propaganda with better graphics, but doing three honest things:

- Say what you’re doing and why.  
- Show what the other side is doing.  
- Accept that people will inspect your words and your evidence.

Used against China, that looks like this:

- Test Beijing’s “win‑win” language against actual behavior in the South China Sea, trade, and diplomacy.  
- Give other countries real material—imagery, legal arguments, timelines—to base their policies and statements on.  
- Get ahead of disinformation by giving your own public a clear narrative before fake ones settle in.

China’s information playbook banks on people being confused and tired. 

Transparency is the opposite of that: it makes things harder to deny and harder to forget.

🟥 WHAT CHINA’S REACTIONS ARE TELLING US  

So what do we actually learn about China when we keep criticizing them and don’t let up?

1. We learn the ambitions are hard and non‑negotiable. 

The South China Sea story under Duterte is proof. Manila bent over backwards to be friendly, distanced itself from the US, and played down the arbitration win. 

Even then, Chinese ships kept harassing our vessels and fishermen. 

In one case, a Chinese naval ship pointed its gun control director at a Philippine Navy vessel just days after Duterte announced he was suspending a major defense deal with Washington. 

That isn’t a small miscommunication. That’s a signal: “Your concessions don’t matter. Our claims do.”

2. We learn coercion is a habit, not an exception. 

The EU and Australia didn’t do anything crazy. One imposed narrow sanctions over human rights; the other asked for an investigation into a global pandemic. 

The reply from Beijing was the same toolbox: trade pressure, sanctions, loud diplomatic blowback. 

Researchers have logged well over a hundred such cases of economic coercion in the last decade. 

This tells smaller countries: if you engage with China, expect your economy and diaspora to be used as levers.

3. We see institutional weaknesses. 

Analyses of Chinese crisis management show internal tensions: diplomats might prefer one path, the military another; central leaders say one thing, local actors push in another direction. 

Propaganda needs can push toward escalation just to show “strength,” even when caution would serve long‑term interests better. That mix creates space for miscalculation.

4. We see the gap between story and reality. 

China talks endlessly about international law and order, but rejects unfavorable rulings, draws absurd lines on maps, and extends its domestic laws into waters that aren’t legally its own. 

Each recorded incident in the West Philippine Sea adds another example to that gap. Over time, other states stop hearing “responsible great power” and start seeing “serial rule‑bender.”

If we say nothing, all of this can be spun away as isolated incidents. 

If we keep criticizing, documenting, and explaining, the pattern becomes impossible to ignore.

🟥 THE RISK AND THE RESPONSIBILITY  

There’s a personal tension here I can’t ignore.

I dream of us, as a country, to be braver. I hope for  us to name Chinese actions as illegal when they’re illegal. I dream of us to stop letting ourselves be treated like we’re overreacting, to refuse to swallow carefully managed narratives that make us feel small or unreasonable. 

I believe secrecy and “wag na lang natin palakihin” mainly protect the stronger side.

But I also know who gets caught in the middle when things get hot. It’s not the people writing foreign ministry notes. 

It’s sailors on small ships, fishers who suddenly can’t enter waters their families have depended on for generations, communities whose economies crack under the weight of crisis, and Filipinos who are living and working in China. 

That’s why the line “keep criticizing China and do not let up” has to be paired with another line: keep your head. 

The job is not to perform anger for the algorithm. The job is to tell the truth precisely, consistently, and in a way that supports real strategy, not just emotion.

There’s a narrow path between being too scared to speak and being so loud you push things toward a fight you’re not ready for. 

The discipline is to walk that path anyway.

🟥 WHY WE CAN’T BACK OFF NOW  

In the end, this is the real choice: either we live under China’s story of what’s happening, or we insist on keeping our own record.

If we stay quiet, there will be fewer documents, fewer videos, fewer clear arguments for future Filipinos to look back on and say, “Ito ang ginawa nila, at ito ang sinabi natin.” That kind of silence is comfortable in the short term and brutal in the long term.

China has picked its model: heavy nationalism, opaque decision‑making, aggressive reaction to criticism. 

We can’t change that from here. 

What we can do is use the one edge we actually have: the ability to talk, write, publish, argue, leak, and repeat until the truth becomes harder to bury.

That’s why I keep coming back to the same conclusion: keep criticizing China and do not let up. 

Not with lazy insults, but with facts, stories, names, dates, and patterns. 

Every time they overreact, they help prove the point. Every time they punish someone for speaking, they underline exactly why that speech was necessary.

If we do our part right, their own reactions will keep exposing what their slogans are trying to hide.
